notes: The central relationship of the entire canon. Holmes and Watson meet in 1881 and share lodgings at 221B Baker Street. Watson serves as Holmes's companion, assistant, and chronicler. The relationship is asymmetrical — Holmes the genius, Watson the capable everyman — but Watson's loyalty and willingness to follow Holmes into danger make him irreplaceable. Holmes expresses his regard obliquely: through trust and the fact that Watson is the only person he consistently chooses to have near him.

The defining antagonistic relationship of the canon. Holmes describes Moriarty as his intellectual equal and polar opposite — genius deployed for criminal organization rather than detection. Their relationship is characterized by mutual recognition of the other's abilities beneath total enmity. They meet in person only twice in the canonical text.

Adler is the one person who has unambiguously bested Holmes. He refers to her as 'The Woman' — using the definite article to signal her unique status. Watson notes Holmes's regard for her was not love (he distrusted emotions as interfering with reason) but something distinct from his regard for anyone else. They never meet again after A Scandal in Bohemia.

Mycroft is Sherlock's older brother. Holmes states plainly that Mycroft's powers of deduction exceed his own but that Mycroft entirely lacks the energy to apply them actively. Affectionate but characteristically understated — they meet infrequently, communicate efficiently, and respect each other's distinct spheres. Mycroft occasionally provides government intelligence or calls on Holmes when his own sedentary nature prevents him from acting.

A long professional relationship built on grudging mutual respect. Lestrade represents the best of the official police but lacks Holmes's imaginative leaps. He consults Holmes while publicly maintaining skepticism. Holmes allows him to take public credit for solutions, understanding Lestrade's career depends on visible results. By the later stories Lestrade's skepticism has mellowed into genuine admiration.

Watson meets Mary Morstan when she arrives at 221B as a client in The Sign of the Four. He falls for her immediately — describing her with the warmth conspicuously absent from his prose elsewhere — and they marry shortly after the case concludes. Mary's death (occurring off-page between stories) brings Watson back to Baker Street. She is the one significant domestic attachment of his life, and her absence in the later canon shapes his return to Holmes's orbit.

Holmes is Mrs. Hudson's most famous — and most exasperating — tenant. He pays well and she tolerates his chemistry experiments, erratic hours, revolver practice against the wall, and the constant parade of unusual visitors. Holmes is more attached to her than his manner suggests: in The Adventure of the Empty House he recruits her to slowly manipulate a wax bust in front of the window, a task placing her in a sniper's line of sight. She agrees without hesitation. In His Last Bow she is identified by her first name Martha, and is revealed to have served Holmes during a covert mission.

Watson shares lodgings at 221B and occupies a secondary-tenant relationship with Mrs. Hudson. It is warmer and more straightforward than Holmes's: Watson appreciates her domestic steadiness and she is spared the worst of the eccentricities that Holmes alone generates. Watson notes her patience admiringly more than once. When he marries and moves out, she remains in the building and in the background of his life whenever he returns to Baker Street.

Moran is Holmes's primary adversary in the period immediately after Reichenbach — the one survivor of Moriarty's network capable of threatening him. Holmes spent three years abroad partly to avoid Moran, who had tried to finish him in the Reichenbach gorge. When Holmes returns to London in 1894, he engineers Moran's arrest by using a wax bust as bait in the window of 221B. Moran attempts to shoot it from Camden House across the street; Holmes catches him in the act. It is a neat mirror of the Moriarty trap: this time Holmes controls the scene entirely.

Moran is Moriarty's most trusted lieutenant and the network's chief enforcer. Holmes's dossier on Moran describes him as the second most dangerous man in London — the first being Moriarty. After Moriarty's death at Reichenbach, Moran continues operating independently, motivated partly by personal vendetta against Holmes. The relationship is the defining villain-cluster edge in the canon: Moriarty supplies the intellect, Moran the lethal capability.

Holmes regards Gregson as one of the two best men at Scotland Yard — he and Lestrade form the top tier of official detection. Gregson is more willing than Lestrade to acknowledge Holmes openly and to ask for help without the performance of skepticism. Holmes assists him in Study in Scarlet, Five Orange Pips, and Wisteria Lodge. The relationship is cooperative but never warm — Holmes is a consultant, Gregson an official, and neither pretends otherwise.

Holmes takes an unusual interest in Hopkins, explicitly describing him as a promising officer who has the makings of a great detective. Across their three cases together Holmes functions as a teacher — correcting Hopkins's reasoning, demonstrating the observational method, and showing patience with errors he would dismiss more curtly in Lestrade or Gregson. Hopkins represents the possibility that official detection could improve if it adopted Holmes's approach, which gives the relationship a different quality to Holmes's other Yarder connections.

Lestrade and Gregson are colleagues at Scotland Yard and rivals for professional distinction. In A Study in Scarlet they work the same case independently, each certain his approach is superior, each seeking credit. Holmes observes their competition with dry amusement and notes that they are each as eager to outshine the other as to solve the case. Their relationship is the institutional face of Scotland Yard's limitations: competent men in competition rather than cooperation, neither willing to build on the other's work.

Lestrade and Hopkins are both Scotland Yard inspectors working roughly the same era, representing a generational contrast: Lestrade experienced and entrenched in his methods, Hopkins younger and more open to Holmes's influence. They appear in overlapping but non-identical cases within the Return and do not share scenes in the canonical text. Their relationship is inferred as one of professional hierarchy rather than personal connection.

Henry Baskerville engages Holmes to investigate the threat against his life after arriving in England to claim his inheritance. Holmes takes the case seriously enough to send Watson ahead to Dartmoor while continuing his own investigations in London. Henry is a cooperative client despite finding the restrictions Holmes places on his movements frustrating. Holmes ultimately saves his life, though not before the case comes dangerously close to killing him on the moor.

Holmes sends Watson to Dartmoor as Henry's companion and protector while he continues his own investigation in London. Watson becomes Henry's closest ally on the moor — accompanying him to Baskerville Hall, observing the neighbors, and sending Holmes detailed reports. The relationship is that of a professional guardian and a frightened client: Watson is loyal to Holmes's instructions while becoming genuinely fond of Henry's directness and courage. Henry is one of Watson's more substantial solo-narrative responsibilities in the entire canon.

Roylott visits Baker Street before Holmes has finished his investigation — an unusual reversal where the villain confronts the detective directly. He physically intimidates Holmes, bending a steel fireplace poker with his bare hands and threatening him with unspecified consequences. Holmes straightens the poker and remarks that Roylott seems to have shown his strength. Watson notes that Holmes lists the Speckled Band case among the few in which he faced genuine physical danger to his person. Roylott dies by his own method before Holmes can bring a formal case.

Stapleton is aware of Holmes's investigation earlier than Holmes realizes — he has been watching the Baskerville situation and correctly suspects when a detective arrives that the game is endangered. He and Holmes meet as apparent neighbors, with Stapleton playing the part of a friendly naturalist while Holmes suspects him. The relationship is one of mutual concealment: Stapleton trying to hide his identity and plan, Holmes trying to conceal how much he knows. When Holmes sets the final trap in chapter 13, Stapleton attempts to flee through Grimpen Mire rather than face capture, and is presumed drowned.

Holmes hunts Hope from the Lauriston Gardens crime scene without knowing his identity, piecing together the killer's physical description from footprints, traces, and the RACHE inscription. He catches Hope in a neat trap: advertising the lost ring and waiting for the owner to claim it. Despite the adversarial framing, Holmes respects Hope — recognizing a man driven by genuine injustice rather than base greed. He allows Hope to tell his full story before his death. Watson notes Holmes's quiet admiration for Hope's determination across two decades of pursuit.

Holmes reconstructs Small from the crime scene at Pondicherry Lodge — identifying the wooden leg, the small companion Tonga, and the connection to the Agra pact — then organizes the Thames boat chase that ends in Small's capture. Small is hostile but not without dignity: he gives Holmes a full account of his history once caught, and Holmes listens without contempt. Small's decision to throw the treasure into the Thames rather than allow it to fall to the Sholto family is one Holmes implicitly understands as a consistent act from a consistent man.

Holmes's loathing of Milverton is unusually personal and stated. He calls him the worst man in London — a judgment that places the blackmailer above Moriarty in terms of moral culpability, because Moriarty's criminality springs from ambition while Milverton's springs from indifference to human suffering. Holmes goes so far as to break into Appledore Towers — a clear criminal act — to recover letters held over a client. When he witnesses Milverton's murder by a woman he cannot identify, Holmes refuses to help the police, and Watson records that he destroyed the evidence. It is the starkest case of Holmes choosing personal moral judgment over legal obligation.

Helen Stoner arrives at Baker Street at dawn in a state of visible fear, and Holmes takes the case immediately — one of the few instances in the canon where he does not question the client's credibility before acting. He travels to Stoke Moran to investigate, spends a night in Julia's old room, and confronts the danger directly. Helen's courage in coming to Baker Street at all — given Roylott's violence — impresses Holmes. The case is one of the clearest examples in the canon of Holmes using his abilities explicitly to protect a client rather than simply to solve a puzzle.

Holmes's interest in Violet Hunter is noticeably warmer than his usual client relations — Watson observes it with mild surprise. Holmes advises her against taking the Copper Beeches position but does not prevent her, and she proves herself entirely capable of gathering the intelligence Holmes needs once she is inside the house. He rushes to her aid the moment she sends for help. Watson records that Holmes followed her subsequent career with interest; she becomes a headmistress, one of the few clients whose later life is noted in the canon.

Holmes and Norton meet only once, under unusual circumstances: Holmes is following Irene Adler when he and Norton both arrive at a church, and Norton — frantic because he needs a witness to marry Adler legally — presses Holmes into service. Holmes agrees. It is one of the stranger moments in the canon: Holmes unwittingly facilitating the marriage that removes Adler from the King of Bohemia's reach and defeats his own mission. He describes Norton afterward with a trace of rueful respect — the man moved quickly and got what he wanted.

Irene Adler and Godfrey Norton marry during the events of A Scandal in Bohemia — with Sherlock Holmes as an unwitting witness to the ceremony. They depart England for the Continent immediately after, rendering Holmes's mission moot and placing both characters beyond the reach of any further intrigue. The marriage is presented as genuine rather than tactical: they had already been meeting regularly before the ceremony, and Norton's urgency to marry suggests he feared Adler's safety more than he feared delay. Watson notes it in passing; Holmes, characteristically, does not comment on it directly.

Violet Smith consults Holmes about the mysterious cyclist who follows her weekly. Holmes takes the case but handles it with less than his usual urgency — he sends Watson to observe first and acts too slowly to prevent a forced marriage ceremony. He later acknowledges the error. Despite this uncharacteristic lapse, he resolves the situation and the forced marriage is annulled. Watson's narration is unusually warm about Violet Smith's appearance and manner, a detail Holmes notes with amusement.

Mary Sutherland hires Holmes to find her missing fiancé Hosmer Angel. Holmes solves the case rapidly — the fiancé was a fiction created by her stepfather Windibank — but takes the notable decision not to tell her. He reasons she would not believe him, that the truth would cause her more pain than the comfortable uncertainty she already lives with, and that Windibank will receive his own punishment from the universe in due course. It is one of the clearest examples in the canon of Holmes choosing the client's emotional welfare over full disclosure — a paternalistic judgment that Watson neither endorses nor challenges.
